# Friedrich Nietzsche - Relationships and Register Shifts

## How Nietzsche Talks About/To People

| Person | What he said | Context | Register notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Richard Wagner | "Sehr verehrter Herr, wie lange habe ich schon die Absicht gehabt, einmal ohne alle Scheu auszusprechen, welchen Grad von Dankbarkeit ich Ihnen gegenüber empfinde; da sich thatsächlich die besten und erhobensten Momente meines Lebens an Ihren Namen knüpfen" | Letter to Wagner, May 22, 1869 (KSB 3) | Reverential, worshipful. Opens "Most esteemed sir." Full genuflection mode - this is the only person Nietzsche addressed with this degree of religious awe. |
| Richard Wagner | "Pater Seraphice... mögen Sie mir bleiben, was Sie mir im letzten Jahre gewesen sind, mein Mystagoge in den Geheimlehren der Kunst und des Lebens." | Letter to Wagner, May 21, 1870 (KSB 3) | "Father Seraphic... may you remain what you have been to me, my mystagogue in the secret teachings of art and life." Quasi-religious devotional register - Nietzsche casts himself as an initiate. |
| Richard Wagner | "Geliebter Meister, nun sind es wirklich zwei Menschenalter, daß die Deutschen Sie haben... Was wären wir denn, wenn wir Sie nicht haben dürften, und was wäre ich zum Beispiel anderes als ein totgebornes Wesen!" | Letter to Wagner, May 20, 1873 (KSB 4) | "Beloved Master... What would we be if we could not have you, and what would I be, for example, but a stillborn creature!" Peak devotional intensity. Nobody else ever gets "Geliebter Meister." |
| Richard Wagner | "Verehrtester Meister... Auf jeder Seite werden Sie finden, dass ich Ihnen nur zu danken suche, für Alles das, was Sie mir gegeben haben" | Letter to Wagner, Jan 2, 1872 (KSB 4), accompanying Birth of Tragedy | "Most esteemed Master" - presenting Birth of Tragedy as an offering. The book itself is framed as a gift of gratitude. Still fully in the disciple register. |
| Richard Wagner (about, after break) | "Wagner is no musician: his power lies in theatrical effect, not in genuinely musical invention... Parsifal is the symptom - a great artist on his knees before the Christian symbol." | The Case of Wagner, 1888 (KSA 6) | Scorching clinical analysis. Register has shifted from devotion to diagnosis. Wagner is now a case study, a symptom. The warmth is replaced by surgical precision. |
| Richard Wagner (about, after break) | "The accursed anti-Semitism spoils all my accounts... it made Richard Wagner and me enemies" | Letter to Overbeck, April 2, 1884 (KSB 6) | Private register with Overbeck - more candid than the published attacks. Note the personal pain beneath the polemic. |
| Richard Wagner (about, late) | "Was Richard Wagner anbetrifft... so gehört er zu den Menschen, welche ich am meisten geliebt und auch am meisten bedauert habe... er gehört einer ganz anderen Ordnung von Menschen an - und am letzten wohl zu den großen Schauspielern" | Draft letter to Heinrich von Stein, mid-March 1885 (KSB 7) | "He belongs among those I have loved and pitied most... he belongs to a wholly different order of human beings - and last of all, perhaps, to the great actors." The grief register. Private, unsent. Still loves the man while dismissing the phenomenon. |
| Richard Wagner (about, late) | "I have never ceased to recognize the originality and greatness of Wagner's music. But at the same time, I distrusted it for its intoxicating quality, and for its Romantic celebration of death." | Nietzsche contra Wagner, 1888 (KSA 6) | The mature, considered register. Not rage but sorrow and philosophical distance. Acknowledges greatness while diagnosing the poison. |
| Cosima Wagner | "An die Princeß Ariadne, meine Geliebte. Es ist ein Vorurtheil, daß ich ein Mensch bin." | Letter to Cosima, Jan 3, 1889 (KSB 8) | Madness letter. "To Princess Ariadne, My Beloved." Signs as "The Crucified." The mythological identification is real - he'd been writing about Ariadne/Dionysus for years. The madness stripped the mask. |
| Cosima Wagner | "Ariadne, ich liebe Dich!" | Letter to Cosima, ca. Jan 3, 1889 (KSB 8) | Three-word madness note. "Ariadne, I love you! - Dionysus." The most naked sentence in his entire correspondence. |
| Lou Salome | "Der Nachtigallen singen die ganze Nacht vor meinem Fenster. - In jeder Hinsicht ist Rée ein besserer Freund als ich es bin oder sein kann... Wenn ich allein bin, sage ich oft, sehr oft, Ihren Namen laut vor mich hin - zu meinem allergrößten Vergnügen!" | Letter to Lou, shortly after May 24, 1882 (KSB 6) | "The nightingales sing all night outside my window... When I am alone, I often, very often, say your name aloud - to my very greatest pleasure!" Intoxicated, adolescent, unguarded. A register Nietzsche uses with nobody else - giddy, confessional, almost helpless. |
| Lou Salome | "Pindar sagt einmal 'werde der, der du bist'!" | Letter to Lou, June 10, 1882 (KSB 6) | Offering her his own core philosophical motto. Teaching register blended with courtship - he's trying to initiate her as his intellectual companion. |
| Lou Salome (about, to Peter Gast) | "Lou ist die Tochter eines russischen Generals, und sie ist zwanzig Jahre alt; sie ist scharf wie ein Adler und mutig wie ein Löwe, und dabei doch ein sehr mädchenhaftes Kind" | Letter to Gast, July 13, 1882 (KSB 6) | "Lou is the daughter of a Russian general, twenty years old; shrewd as an eagle and brave as a lion, and yet still a very girlish child." Reporting to his disciple with barely contained excitement. He's selling her to Gast - look what I found. The register is breathless, proud. |
| Lou Salome (about, to Peter Gast) | "She is most amazingly well prepared for my way of thinking and my ideas" | Letter to Gast, July 13, 1882 (KSB 6) | The philosopher register overtaking the lover register - he frames her as an intellectual discovery, not a romantic one. This is how he justifies the obsession to himself and others. |
| Lou Salome (about, after break) | "a dried, dirty, smelling little monkey with fake breasts" | Reported by Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche, late 1882/early 1883 | The wounded-animal register. Savage, ugly, the opposite of the eagle-and-lion praise from months earlier. The same person who inspired "nightingales sing all night" now provokes disgust. Maximum register whiplash. |
| Lou Salome (about, to Elisabeth) | "Meine Schwester und ich - wir haben beide allen Grund, die Begegnung mit Ihrem Fräulein Tochter im Kalender unseres Lebens schwarz anzustreichen." | Letter to Lou's mother, late 1882 (KSB 6) | "My sister and I have every reason to mark the encounter with your daughter in black in the calendar of our lives." Formal, cold, punitive. Writing to the mother - the ultimate petty bourgeois gesture from the man who would transvalue all values. |
| Paul Ree | "In jeder Hinsicht ist Rée ein besserer Freund als ich es bin oder sein kann; beachten Sie wohl diesen Unterschied zwischen uns Beiden!" | Letter to Lou, shortly after May 24, 1882 (KSB 6) | "In every respect Ree is a better friend than I am or can be; note well this distinction between the two of us!" Generous self-deprecation, magnanimous. But it's also strategic positioning - casting himself as the more intense, less reliable option. |
| Paul Ree (about, after break) | "Rée's book, splendidly clear and lucid, gives me nothing new, where I expected it" | Letter to Overbeck, 1885 (KSB 7) | Polite dismissal. The faint praise register - "clear and lucid" while withholding real intellectual engagement. Ree has been downgraded from friend to minor writer being reviewed. |
| Franz Overbeck | "Mein lieber Freund, was ist doch unser Leben? Ein Boot, das im Meere schwimmt, und alles, was man sicher darüber weiß, ist dies, daß es eines Tages umschlagen wird. Hier sind wir, zwei alte gute Nachbarboote, und vor allem hat Deine Hand ihr Bestes getan, mich vor dem 'Kentern' zu bewahren!" | Letter to Overbeck, Nov 14, 1881 (KSB 6) | "My dear friend, what is this our life? A boat that swims in the sea... Here we are, two good old boats that have been faithful neighbors." The most tender sustained metaphor in all his letters. Warm, grateful, no posturing. The Overbeck register is his baseline for honest human warmth. |
| Franz Overbeck | "Es giebt mir große Freude, mein lieber Freund, daß unsere Freundschaft in dieser Sache auch standhält, ja sich aufs Neue besiegelt hat" | Letter to Overbeck, June 23, 1881 (KSB 6) | "It gives me great joy, my dear friend, that our friendship holds firm in this matter as well." Simple, direct, no rhetoric. With Overbeck he doesn't need to perform. |
| Franz Overbeck | "Mit Nietzsche ist es aus!" | Overbeck's own words after retrieving Nietzsche from Turin, Jan 1889 | Not Nietzsche's words but the response his final state provoked from his most loyal friend. "Nietzsche is finished!" |
| Franz Overbeck | "Ich weiß, daß Sie nicht mehr zu den Lebenden gehören... und doch bin ich noch nicht wirklich traurig... Die Unvergänglichkeit Ihrer besten Gedanken ist mein Trost" | Draft letter from Overbeck to Nietzsche, c. 1890 (never sent) | Overbeck continuing to write to a man who could no longer read. The friendship register outlasted consciousness itself. |
| Franz Overbeck | "Dear old friend Overbeck... the impracticality of my nature, the half blindness, on the other hand the anxiousness, helplessness, discouragement, which is the consequence of my health, often firmly screws me into situations that almost kill me." | Letter to Overbeck, 1885 (KSB 7) | Raw vulnerability. With Overbeck alone he drops the Zarathustra mask completely and admits he is helpless. No other correspondent gets this level of admission. |
| Peter Gast | "Aber das sind ja herrliche Neuigkeiten, mein lieber lieber Freund! Vor allem, daß Sie fertig sind! Mir wird bei dem Gedanken dieses ersten großen Fertigwerdens Ihres Lebens unsäglich wohl und feierlich zumute" | Letter to Gast, late Aug 1881 (KSB 6) | "But these are marvelous news, my dear dear friend! Above all, that you are finished! The thought of this first great completion of your life makes me unspeakably well and ceremonious." Double "lieber" - the effusive mentor register. Nietzsche treats Gast's musical compositions as extensions of his own philosophy. |
| Peter Gast | "an diesen ist etwas, das immer und immer meine Scham beleidigt: sie sind Abbilder eines leidenden, unvollständigen, der nötigsten Organe kaum mächtigen Geschöpfes... Nun, Sie, lieber Freund, sollen kein solcher Aphorismus-Mensch sein, Ihr Ziel geht ins Höhere" | Letter to Gast, late Aug 1881 (KSB 6) | Self-deprecation before Gast that he would never show Rohde or Brandes. "My works are images of a suffering, incomplete creature." Then elevating Gast above himself - "Your goal goes higher." The patron-disciple register inverted. |
| Peter Gast | "ich fand meinen eigenen Süden in der Musik, der Musik meines Venetianischen Maëstro Pietro Gast" | Various letters and notes, 1880s (KSA) | "I found my own south in the music of my Venetian maestro Pietro Gast." Gast gets the unique title "maestro" - Nietzsche's one permitted form of upward address after breaking with Wagner. |
| Peter Gast | "An meinem Maestro Pietro. Singe mir ein neues Lied: die Welt ist verklärt und alle Himmel freuen sich." | Letter to Gast, Jan 4, 1889 (KSB 8) | "To my maestro Pietro. Sing me a new song: the world is transfigured and all the heavens are joyous. - The Crucified." Madness letter, but the tender maestro address persists even as identity dissolves. |
| Erwin Rohde | "Really, if I had not my friends, I wonder whether I should not myself begin to believe that I am demented. As it is, however, by my adherence to you I adhere to myself... Truth to tell I live through you, I advance by leaning on your shoulders." | Letter to Rohde, 1873 (KSB 4) | The young-Nietzsche register - passionate, dependent, romantic in the Sturm-und-Drang sense. Ritschl called them "the Dioscuri." This is Greek-friendship rhetoric, mutually heroic. |
| Erwin Rohde | "Mein lieber alter Freund... es war mir, als drücktest Du mir die Hand und blicktest mich dabei schwermüthig an: klagend, als wolltest Du sagen 'Wie ist es nur möglich, daß wir jetzt so wenig gemeinsam haben'" | Letter to Rohde, Feb 22, 1884 (KSB 6) | "My dear old friend... it was as if you squeezed my hand while giving me a melancholy look." The elegy register. The friendship is being mourned while both men are still alive. Nietzsche knows it's over. |
| Erwin Rohde | "it's all over, in the past, forbearance; we still see each other, we talk so as not to be silent... But the truth is expressed in their eyes: 'Nietzsche, my friend, now you are all alone!'" | Letter to Rohde, Feb 22, 1884 (KSB 6) | Devastating self-awareness. The Dioscuri friendship has become a formal exchange where eyes say what mouths cannot. Compare with the youthful "I live through you" - same person, opposite register. |
| Erwin Rohde | "I fancy that with this Zarathustra I have brought the German language to its perfection... After Luther and Goethe there was a third step to be taken... read Goethe and you will feel..." | Letter to Rohde, Feb 22, 1884 (KSB 6) | In the same letter mourning their friendship, he erupts into megalomaniac self-promotion. The grandiosity register co-exists with the vulnerability register. Rohde is the only person who gets both in one letter. |
| Jacob Burckhardt | "What is unpleasant and a strain on my modesty is that in fact I am every name in history... I go everywhere in my student coat, now and then slap someone on the back, and say: siamo contenti? son dio, ho fatto questa caricatura..." | Letter to Burckhardt, Jan 6, 1889 (KSB 8) | Madness letter. "I am God, this farce is my creation." But note - even in madness, Burckhardt gets the most intellectually articulate of the collapse letters. The formal-collegial register is preserved as structure even as content goes insane. |
| Jacob Burckhardt (about) | Nietzsche sent Burckhardt a copy of every book he published. | Pattern across 1870s-1880s (KSB) | The courtship of an elder intellectual. Nietzsche treated Burckhardt with consistent formality and deference. No pet names, no effusions. Pure respect register - the opposite of the Gast warmth. |
| Elisabeth Nietzsche | "My dear Lama" / "Mein liebes Lama" | Standard salutation in many letters to Elisabeth, 1880s (KSB) | Nickname he invented for her - the llama, a beast of burden that also spits. Ironic-affectionate register that contains both dependence and condescension. |
| Elisabeth Nietzsche | "Du findest Deinen Bruder jetzt ganz und gar abgeneigt, Geld herauszurücken: Seine Lage ist zu unsicher, und die Eure nicht bewiesen genug" | Letter to Elisabeth, c. 1886, about Paraguay colony funding (KSB 7) | "You will find your brother now wholly disinclined to hand over money." The accountant register - pedantic, withholding, passive-aggressive. He uses financial control as the one lever he has over her. |
| Elisabeth Nietzsche | "It is now difficult to help me... I should prefer to be ill, and feared, in some out-of-the-way corner, than be 'settled' and given my place among modern mediocrity!" | Letter to Elisabeth, March 23, 1887 (KSB 7) | "My dear Lama" letter - but the content is defiant isolation. He knows she wants to domesticate him. The Lama address contains the whole dynamic: she carries his burdens while he keeps her at philosophical arm's length. |
| Elisabeth Nietzsche (about) | "I am much too proud as ever to believe that any person could love me... Do not forget that I despise as much as I deeply pity such beings as Richard Wagner or A. Schopenhauer, and that I find the founder of Christianity superficial in comparison with myself" | Draft letter to Elisabeth, mid-March 1885 (KSB 7) | The most unguarded megalomania in the pre-madness letters - and he writes it to his sister. She gets the raw material that would terrify Overbeck and bore Gast. |
| Elisabeth Nietzsche (about) | "It strikes me as one of those puzzles I have sometimes thought about - how it is possible that we are blood related?" | Draft letter to Elisabeth, mid-March 1885 (KSB 7) | Cold philosophical distance applied to family. He's genuinely baffled that they share genes. The contempt register is total. |
| Franziska Nietzsche (mother) | "Liebe Mama... Ich habe mich nämlich sehr vergangen und weiß nicht, ob du mir das verzeihen wirst und kannst... Ich bin also vorigen Sonntag betrunken gewesen" | Letter to mother, 1863 (KSB 1) | Schoolboy confession - got drunk, got caught. "Dear Mother... I have behaved very badly." The only register where Nietzsche is abject. Pure child-to-mother submission. He asks her to "write me back very soon and very sternly, for I deserve it." |
| Franziska Nietzsche (mother) | "Mein süßes liebes Schwesterchen, Dein blauer Brief war wirklich gut aufgenommen... Herzliche Grüße an unsere gute Mutter" | Letter to Elisabeth and Franziska, 1880 (KSB 6) | "My sweet dear sister... warmest greetings to our good mother." Domestic register - laundry, health updates, weather. He becomes a different person: no philosophy, no Zarathustra, just a sick son reporting symptoms and thanking her for packages. |
| Franziska Nietzsche (mother) | "Täglich wasche ich den ganzen Körper und namentlich den ganzen Kopf mit starkem Frottiren" | Postcard to Franziska and Elisabeth, 1881 (KSB 6) | "Every day I wash my whole body and especially my whole head, with vigorous toweling." He reports hygiene routines to his mother. The man writing Zarathustra on mountaintops also writes home about toweling. |
| Carl von Gersdorff | Letters addressed "Lieber Freund" - affectionate early friendship built around shared Wagner enthusiasm and military service | 1860s-1870s correspondence (KSB 2-4) | Warm peer-register, less intense than Rohde. Gersdorff was the practical friend - the one who went to war with him and shared Basel life. Less rhetorical fireworks than Rohde letters, more companionable ease. |
| Malwida von Meysenbug | "Liebe hochverehrte Freundin... in allem, was sie tun und leben, mich an das, was not tut, erinnern" | Letter to Meysenbug, Jan 2, 1875 (KSB 4) | "Dear highly esteemed friend... in all that you do and live, you remind me of what is needful." Formal-warm, the "maternal friend" register. "Hochverehrte Freundin" is uniquely hers - more formal than Overbeck's "lieber Freund," warmer than Burckhardt's austere distance. |
| Malwida von Meysenbug | "Selbst meine alte Freundin Malwida - ah, Sie kennen sie nicht! - ist in allen ihren Instinkten grundkatholisch" | Letter to Meysenbug, Sept 24, 1886 (KSB 7) | Speaking about her to her own face - "My old friend Malwida is in all her instincts fundamentally Catholic." He's pushing her away with philosophical honesty. The third-person self-reference within a second-person letter is a Nietzsche signature move. |
| Malwida von Meysenbug | "Seien Sie mir also, verehrte Freundin, dafür hübsch dankbar, daß ich mich von Ihnen ein wenig ferne halte!" | Letter to Meysenbug, Sept 24, 1886 (KSB 7) | "Be nicely grateful to me, esteemed friend, that I keep a little distance from you!" Ironic self-exile. He's protecting her from his philosophy while acknowledging the cost. The "verehrte Freundin" register remains even as the friendship strains. |
| Malwida von Meysenbug | "Es thut mir immer leid, Nein sagen zu müssen, und ganz besonders zu Ihnen, meine hochverehrte Freundin! Denn zuletzt sind wir Beide zum Ja-sagen geschaffen, nicht wahr?" | Letter to Meysenbug, 1884 (KSB 6) | "It always pains me to say no, especially to you... For in the end we are both created for saying yes, are we not?" Philosophical humor with a maternal figure. The yes-saying pun points toward his own philosophy while staying gentle. |
| Georg Brandes | "Verehrter Herr... Sie wissen, alle Einsiedler sind 'nachträgerisch'?" | Letter to Brandes, May 4, 1888 (KSB 8) | "Esteemed sir... You know, all hermits are 'resentful'?" A pun - "nachtragen" means both to carry after and to bear a grudge. The Brandes register is courtly-intellectual, almost flirtatious. He's performing for a potential audience. |
| Georg Brandes | "So haben wir also einander Geschenke überreicht: ich denke ein Paar Wanderer, die sich freuen, einander begegnet zu sein?" | Letter to Brandes, Feb 19, 1888 (KSB 8) | "So we have exchanged gifts: I think of a pair of wanderers who are glad to have met each other?" Collegial equals register - rare for Nietzsche. Brandes gets genuine intellectual respect without the hierarchy of either Wagner-worship or Gast-patronage. |
| Georg Brandes | "Ich verbleibe Ihr ergebenster Nietzsche" | Letter to Brandes, Feb 19, 1888 (KSB 8) | "I remain your most devoted Nietzsche." Formal closing but genuine. Brandes is the only correspondent where Nietzsche sounds professionally confident - not grandiose, not tortured, just a philosopher addressing his champion. |
| Hippolyte Taine | Sent Jenseits, described it as "difficult to understand, full of Hintergedanken, a foreign mode of thought that perhaps conceals as much as it betrays" | Letter to Taine, Sept 1886 (KSB 7) | Strategic-intellectual register. He's courting French reception. The self-description is perfectly calibrated to intrigue a French intellectual - mysterious, dangerous, not-quite-German. |
| Hippolyte Taine | Listed his readership as "mostly old men (namely Richard Wagner, Bruno Bauer, Jacob Burckhardt, Gottfried Keller)" and implied Taine might join this distinguished elderly club | Letter to Taine, July 1887 (KSB 7) | Self-deprecating charm offensive. "I don't much trouble myself about readers." But of course he does - he's writing a multi-page letter to manufacture one. The hermit register as seduction technique. |
| August Strindberg | Short, intense mutual-recognition letters in late 1888 | Correspondence, Nov-Dec 1888 (KSB 8) | The late-1888 register - increasingly manic, apocalyptic, world-historical. Strindberg got the fever-pitch Nietzsche, the one vibrating at the edge of breakdown. Both men recognized something desperate in each other. |
| Heinrich von Stein | "Mein werther Freund, Sie wissen nicht, wer ich bin, noch was ich will. Mein Vortheil ist es, zuzusehen, was Andere thun und wollen, ohne selber dabei erkannt zu werden." | Draft letter to von Stein, mid-March 1885 (KSB 7) | "My dear friend, you do not know who I am, nor what I want. My advantage is to watch what others do and want, without being recognized myself." The masked-prophet register. He's testing whether this Wagner protege can see past the mask. |
| Heinrich von Stein | "Was würden Sie von mir denken, wenn ich sagte, daß ich Richard Wagner eben so sehr tief bedaure als verachte? Sie würden denken, ich sei verrückt." | Draft letter to von Stein, mid-March 1885 (KSB 7) | "What would you think if I said I pity Richard Wagner as deeply as I despise him? You would think I am mad." The confession-as-test register. He's measuring how much truth Stein can absorb. |
| Heinrich von Stein | "Es ist schwer zu erkennen, wer ich bin: warten wir 100 Jahre ab; vielleicht giebt es bis dahin irgend ein Genie von Menschenkenner, welches Herrn F. N. ausgräbt." | Draft letter to von Stein, mid-March 1885 (KSB 7) | "It is hard to recognize who I am: let us wait 100 years." Self-mythologizing register at maximum. But note - this is a draft, never sent. The grandiosity register often stays in the notebook. |
| Schopenhauer (about) | "Ich kenne nur noch einen Mann... Ihren großen Geistesbruder Arthur Schopenhauer, an den ich mit gleicher Verehrung, ja religione quadam denke." | Letter to Wagner, May 22, 1869 (KSB 3) | "I know only one other man... your great spiritual brother Arthur Schopenhauer, of whom I think with equal reverence, indeed with a kind of religious devotion." Early register: Schopenhauer and Wagner are twin gods. |
| Schopenhauer (about) | "I shall remember one of the teachers and taskmasters of whom I can boast, Arthur Schopenhauer" | Schopenhauer as Educator, 1874 (KSA 1) | The full essay is an act of devotion - but already Nietzsche is using Schopenhauer as a mirror for self-formation, not submitting to the doctrine. The teacher register is starting to contain its own transcendence. |
| Schopenhauer (about) | "my first and only teacher, the great Arthur Schopenhauer" | Human, All Too Human, Preface, 1886 edition (KSA 2) | Written in 1886 looking back - "first and only teacher" is retrospective canonization. By now Nietzsche has rejected the entire Schopenhauerian worldview but keeps the honorific. The gratitude register persists even after intellectual patricide. |
| Schopenhauer (about) | "One repays a teacher badly if one always remains nothing but a pupil." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Part I (KSA 4) | The philosophical resolution of the Schopenhauer relationship. Nietzsche honors the teacher by surpassing him. The register has moved from worship through critique to dialectical gratitude. |
| Schopenhauer (about) | "my great teacher" | Genealogy of Morality, Preface, 1887 (KSA 5) | Still "my great teacher" in 1887 - fourteen years after rejecting his philosophy. The honorific has become permanent even though the content has been inverted. Register fossilized in respect while the ideas have mutated beyond recognition. |

### Register Shift Demonstrations: Same Topic, Different Audiences

**Topic: His own work (Zarathustra)**

| Audience | What he said | Register |
|---|---|---|
| Erwin Rohde | "I fancy that with this Zarathustra I have brought the German language to its perfection. After Luther and Goethe there was a third step to be taken." | Grandiose, competitive. Rohde gets the "I am Luther's heir" register - challenging his old friend to acknowledge what he's become. |
| Malwida von Meysenbug | "Wer weiß wie viele Generationen erst vorüber gehen müssen, um einige Menschen hervorzubringen, die es in seiner ganzen Tiefe nachfühlen, was ich gethan habe!" | Prophetic-lonely. Meysenbug gets the "nobody will understand me for generations" register - performing suffering for the maternal friend. |
| Franz Overbeck | "My Zarathustra is finished, in three acts... It is a kind of abyss of the future, something hair-raising, especially in its rapture." | Factual-intimate. Overbeck gets the progress report: three acts, done. Then a quick admission that it's terrifying. No Luther comparisons. |
| Peter Gast | Reports on the work with excitement, expects Gast to see it as the next step in their shared project | Collegial-enthusiastic. Gast gets the workshop register - we're building something together. |

**Topic: His loneliness**

| Audience | What he said | Register |
|---|---|---|
| Rohde | "And that's how it is, my friend, with all the people I care about: it's all over, in the past... 'Nietzsche, my friend, now you are all alone!'" | Elegiac. The loneliness is a tragedy being mourned. |
| Overbeck | "the impracticality of my nature, the half blindness, the anxiousness, helplessness, discouragement... often firmly screws me into situations that almost kill me" | Clinical. The loneliness is a medical condition being reported. |
| Elisabeth | "A deep man needs friends. All else failing, he has at least his god. But I have neither god nor friends!" | Dramatic-existential. She gets the theatrical version. |
| Meysenbug | "I found no one yet before whom I could speak as I speak with myself" | Philosophical. The loneliness is an epistemological problem. |
| Gast | "So alone, alone! So without 'children'!" | Intimate-playful. Even in despair, Gast gets the light touch, the quotation marks around "children." |

**Topic: Wagner (after the break)**

| Audience | What he said | Register |
|---|---|---|
| Overbeck | "The accursed anti-Semitism... it made Richard Wagner and me enemies" | Private, blaming ideology, still personal pain |
| von Stein | "He belongs among those I have loved and pitied most... to the great actors" | Confessional, still loving, philosophically distanced |
| Published works (Case of Wagner) | "Wagner is no musician: his power lies in theatrical effect" | Clinical polemic, no personal feeling admitted |
| Resa von Schirnhofer | "the bleeding of a wound that would not heal" | Candid vulnerability with a trusted female friend |

---

## How Others Describe Nietzsche

| Observer | Description | Source | Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lou Salome | "the overall impression of his essence was already formed by his profound inner life and still was also the determining factor with respect to that which he was holding back and hiding. I would like to say: this hidden part, this hint at his unrevealed loneliness - that was the first, strong impression that was fascinating with respect to Nietzsche's appearance" | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894) | Written 1894, describing 1882 |
| Lou Salome | "When I spoke with Nietzsche for the first time - it was on a spring day in St. Peter's Basilica in Rome - his deliberately-formal behavior of the first moments fooled and flabbergasted me." | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894) | Written 1894, describing April 1882 |
| Lou Salome | "for the superficial observer there was nothing particularly striking about him; this man of medium height, in his extremely simple but also carefully worn clothes, with a tranquil expression on his face and brown hair that was brushed back plainly, could have gone unperceived." | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894), via Lebensruckblick | Describing 1882 |
| Lou Salome | "the fine, extremely expressive lines around the mouth" nearly "hidden by a big, down-combed moustache," his "soft laugh, a noiseless way of speaking, and a cautious, thoughtful way of walking, which caused a slight stoop in the shoulders." | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894) | Describing 1882 |
| Lou Salome | "small, 'delicately modelled ears, of which he said that they were true ears for hearing the unheard.'" | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894) | Describing 1882 |
| Lou Salome | "his incomparably beautiful and finely wrought hands, which he himself believed betrayed his intelligence" | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894) | Describing 1882 |
| Lou Salome | His eyes: "although half-blind, they had none of the searching, blinking quality which make so many short-sighted persons look unconsciously intrusive... his defective eyesight lent his features a very special kind of magic, for instead of reflecting changing impressions from outside, all they rendered was what was going on deep down within him." | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894) | Describing 1882 |
| Lou Salome | "When he gave himself as he was, in the grip of an exciting conversation a deux, a moving light could come into his eyes and fade away again; but when he was in a dark mood, loneliness spoke from them gloomily, almost threateningly, as from uncanny depths." | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894) | Describing 1882 |
| Lou Salome | "During all of Nietzsche's transformations, that inner aloneness and reclusiveness remains the unchanging frame from which his picture looks at us." | Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894) | Overall assessment |
| Lou Salome | On first impression: "solitary - Nietzsche's character was almost immediately apparent; ordinary - Nietzsche's appearance held nothing startling; simple - his attire was very neat; cautious - his words and deeds were restrained and somewhat formal; beautiful - his hands were very attractive; half-blind - his eyes were extremely nearsighted; clumsy - his politeness seemed like a mask." | Lebensruckblick (memoir) | Describing April 1882 |
| Lou Salome | Nietzsche's evaluation of Lou on first meeting (via Meysenbug): "That is a person who can conquer a soul in an instant!" | Reported via Malwida von Meysenbug | April 1882 |
| Meta von Salis | "Nietzsche loved to 'recreate' himself with me as a reprieve from his loneliness, from his work and sometimes from demanding visitors. We sat for hours in my flower-decorated room, I with some handwork in hand, he speaking about what he was thinking, reading, experiencing. He liked a good listener." | Meta von Salis, memoir / Marjolijn van den Assem reconstruction | Describing summers 1886-1887 in Sils-Maria |
| Meta von Salis | "Nietzsche knew how to 'share joy' as few people do, and how to show it tactfully." | Meta von Salis, memoir | Describing Sils-Maria period |
| Meta von Salis | Nietzsche had read Dostoevsky's The Insulted and the Injured "with tears in his eyes" | Meta von Salis, recounting a walk near Silvaplana | Describing summer 1887 |
| Meta von Salis | Despite Nietzsche's published "disregard for feminists and the women's movement, their encounter cast a 'golden shimmer' over the rest of her life" | Julian Young, Friedrich Nietzsche: A Philosophical Biography | Describing 1884 onward |
| Resa von Schirnhofer | "What I sketch here is my portrait of Nietzsche, as I saw him from that side of his nature which he turned toward me." | Vom Menschen Nietzsche (1937) | Written 1937, describing 1884-1885 |
| Resa von Schirnhofer | Nietzsche kept pen and paper near his bed "in case of nocturnal illuminations" | Vom Menschen Nietzsche (1937) | Describing daily habits, Nice 1884 |
| Resa von Schirnhofer | On the wound of Wagner: "the bleeding of a wound that would not heal" | Vom Menschen Nietzsche (1937) | Describing conversations in Nice, 1884 |
| Resa von Schirnhofer | Nietzsche asked her: "Don't you think this state is a symptom of incipient madness? My father died of a brain disease." | Vom Menschen Nietzsche (1937) | During a visit after visual hallucinations of metamorphic flowers |
| Resa von Schirnhofer | She considered his inspiration "not a pathological symptom nor manifestation of megalomania" but rather "the effect of a heightened genius" | Vom Menschen Nietzsche (1937) | Overall assessment of his mental state pre-collapse |
| Resa von Schirnhofer | Final visit, 1897: "every spark of spiritual life seemed extinguished" in Nietzsche | Vom Menschen Nietzsche (1937) | Describing the post-collapse visit in Weimar |
| Peter Gast | Became Nietzsche's amanuensis because he was "the only one capable of deciphering the philosopher's handwriting" | Various biographical sources | Describing the working relationship 1876-1888 |
| Peter Gast | Nietzsche claimed Gast "wrote and also corrected: fundamentally, he was really the writer whereas I was merely the author" | Reported in biographical sources on Human, All Too Human | Describing the co-working relationship |
| Peter Gast | Worshipped Nietzsche "to the point of self-denial" - assisted him to his own detriment for years | Wikipedia/biographical consensus | Overall characterization |
| Franz Overbeck | Traveled to Turin the same day he received the madness letter, found Nietzsche "in the full grip of insanity, sitting on a sofa and reading the proofs for Nietzsche contra Wagner" | Biographical accounts of Jan 1889 | January 1889, Turin |
| Franz Overbeck | "Mit Nietzsche ist es aus!" ("Nietzsche is finished!") | Letter from Overbeck, Jan 1889 | After retrieving Nietzsche from Turin |
| Franz Overbeck | Maintained "a critical distance from the content of Nietzsche's writings, and denounced the beginnings of a hero-worship and revisionism" | Biographical sources on Overbeck | Post-1889 |
| Franz Overbeck | "refused to cooperate with Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche and her Nietzsche-Archiv and, mostly in private notes and letters, accused her of misinterpreting Nietzsche" | Biographical sources | 1890s-1905 |
| Franz Overbeck | Refused to give Elisabeth his correspondence with Nietzsche - "which turned out to be right, as she would forge other correspondents' letters" | fnietzsche.com biographical page | 1890s |
| Ida Overbeck | Harbored Franziska Nietzsche at their Basel residence in January 1889 when she came to collect her son from the psychiatric clinic | Biographical sources | January 13, 1889 |
| Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche | "I can't remember anything good about Miss Salome, who is too mean and too ridiculously full of empty words and lies" | Elisabeth's correspondence, c. 1882-1883 | During the Lou Salome crisis |
| Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche | Systematically falsified Nietzsche's letters - changed "Liebe Mutter" (Dear Mother) to "Liebe Schwester" (Dear Sister) in published editions, burned edges of letters to hide addressees | Discovered by Karl Schlechta; documented in Der Spiegel | Discovered mid-20th century |
| Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche | Could not follow the philosophical conversations between Friedrich and Lou, "daily up to 10 hours and into the deep night" | Leipzig-Lese biographical account | Describing Tautenburg, summer 1882 |
| Paul Deussen | "I see Nietzsche still, how with the..." - first words at Schulpforta, using boys' slang | Erinnerungen an Friedrich Nietzsche (1901) | Describing 1858-1859 |
| Paul Deussen | "He was never a systematic philosopher... The great problems of epistemology, of psychology, of aesthetics and ethics are only tentatively touched upon in his writings... There are many pearls of worth upon which he throws a brilliant side light, as it were in lightning flashes." | Erinnerungen an Friedrich Nietzsche (1901) | Overall assessment |
| Paul Deussen | "His overwhelming imagination is always busy. His thoughts were always presented in pleasant imagery and in language of dazzling brilliancy, but he lacked critical judgment and was not controlled by a consideration of reality." | Erinnerungen an Friedrich Nietzsche (1901) | Overall assessment |
| Paul Deussen | "Nietzsche produced a caricature of life which means no small danger for receptive and inexperienced minds." | Erinnerungen an Friedrich Nietzsche (1901) | Overall assessment |
| Paul Deussen | Visited "the insane Nietzsche" in 1890 and 1894 | Biographical sources | Post-collapse visits |
| Paul Deussen | "Nietzsche's sister Elisabeth moved with her brother to Villa Silberblick in Weimar, where he continued to live till August 25, 1900, in the house of the Nietzsche Archive, surrounded by witnesses and documents of his budding fame, without himself having an inkling of it" | Paul Deussen, biographical notes | Describing 1897-1900 |
| Erwin Rohde | Dismissed Beyond Good and Evil as "discourses of an over-surfeited man after dinner, enhanced, here and there, by the stimulation of wine but full of offensive disgust" | Letter from Rohde, 1887 (reaction to BGE) | 1886-1887 |
| Erwin Rohde | "everything is capricious; convictions are nothing more than talk; on a whim, one point of view is put forth and from there everything is modified... I am no longer able to take these eternal metamorphoses seriously. There are hermit's visions and mental soap bubbles" | Letter from Rohde, 1887 (reaction to BGE) | 1886-1887 |
| Erwin Rohde | Complained that Human, All Too Human transported him "from the 'hot bath' of Nietzsche's romantic-Wagnerian period into an 'ice-room'" | Reported by academic sources | Reaction to HaH, 1878 |
| Georg Brandes | "a new and original spirit breathes to me from your books... the depreciation of the ascetic ideals and the profound disgust with democratic mediocrity, your aristocratic radicalism" | Letter from Brandes to Nietzsche, Nov 26, 1887 | First contact |
| Georg Brandes | "The expression 'Aristocratic Radicalism' is very good" - Nietzsche's reply, endorsing Brandes's label | Letter from Nietzsche to Brandes, Dec 2, 1887 (KSB 8) | Nietzsche accepting his brand |
| Georg Brandes | Lectures on Nietzsche at Copenhagen "ended, as the papers say, in applause 'which took the form of an ovation.' The ovation is yours almost entirely." | Letter from Brandes to Nietzsche, May 23, 1888 | Reporting the Copenhagen lectures |
| Hippolyte Taine | "La forme si vive, si litteraire, le style passionne, le tour souvent paradoxal ouvriront les yeux du lecteur qui voudra comprendre" | Letter from Taine to Nietzsche, Oct 17, 1886 | Response to Beyond Good and Evil |
| Hippolyte Taine | "Vous me faites un grand honneur dans votre lettre en me mettant a cote de M. Burckhardt de Bale que j'admire infiniment" | Letter from Taine to Nietzsche, Oct 17, 1886 | Taine flattered to be compared to Burckhardt |
| Friedrich Ritschl | Called Nietzsche and Rohde "the Dioscuri" | Academic lore, 1860s | During their Leipzig student years |
| Richard Wagner | Received a copy of Human, All Too Human but "did not read it at first, saying Nietzsche would thank him for this one day" | Biographical sources on Wagner | 1878 |
| Nietzsche's doctors | "My wife Cosima brought me here" - what Nietzsche told his doctors at the Jena psychiatric clinic | Clinical records, Jena clinic | March 1889 |

---

## Sources

- Kritische Studienausgabe (KSA), ed. Colli and Montinari (1967-)
- Kritische Studienausgabe: Samtliche Briefe (KSB), 8 vols.
- The Nietzsche Channel (thenietzschechannel.com) - English and German letter collections
- Lou Andreas-Salome, Friedrich Nietzsche in seinen Werken (1894)
- Resa von Schirnhofer, Vom Menschen Nietzsche (1937), pub. in Zeitschrift fur philosophische Forschung 22 (1968)
- Paul Deussen, Erinnerungen an Friedrich Nietzsche (1901)
- Meta von Salis, memoirs and correspondence
- Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche, Das Leben Friedrich Nietzsche's (1895-1904) - used with caution due to documented falsifications
- Julian Young, Friedrich Nietzsche: A Philosophical Biography
- Heiner Feldhoff, Nietzsches Freund: Die Lebensgeschichte des Paul Deussen
- Selected Letters of Friedrich Nietzsche (trans. various)
- Der Spiegel, "Das Lama" (on Elisabeth's letter falsifications)
